The **Northern War of 1655–1660**[[a]](#cite_note-4) was fought between [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), with participation at different times by [Russia](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia"), [Brandenburg-Prussia](/wiki/Brandenburg-Prussia "Brandenburg-Prussia"), the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), and [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway"). It ended with the treaties of [Copenhagen](/wiki/Treaty_of_Copenhagen_(1660) "Treaty of Copenhagen (1660)") and [Oliva](/wiki/Treaty_of_Oliva "Treaty of Oliva") in 1660.

In 1655, [Charles X](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav_of_Sweden "Charles X Gustav of Sweden") took advantage of the [Russo-Polish War (1654–67)](/wiki/Russo-Polish_War_(1654%E2%80%9367) "Russo-Polish War (1654–67)") to [over-run](/wiki/Deluge_(history) "Deluge (history)") western Poland. The [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania") was [annexed](/wiki/Union_of_K%C4%97dainiai "Union of Kėdainiai") by [Sweden](/wiki/Swedish_Lithuania "Swedish Lithuania"), and [John II Casimir Vasa](/wiki/John_II_Casimir_Vasa "John II Casimir Vasa") took refuge in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"). He managed to regain parts of his kingdom in 1656, and the conflict widened when Russia [declared war on Sweden](/wiki/Russo-Swedish_War_(1656%E2%80%9358) "Russo-Swedish War (1656–58)"), supported by [Emperor Leopold](/wiki/Leopold_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Leopold I, Holy Roman Emperor") and [Frederick III of Denmark](/wiki/Frederick_III_of_Denmark "Frederick III of Denmark").

Previously [allied with Sweden](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%B6nigsberg_(1656) "Treaty of Königsberg (1656)"), Brandenburg switched sides in 1657 when Casimir granted [Frederick William I](/wiki/Frederick_William,_the_Great_Elector "Frederick William, the Great Elector") sovereignty over the [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia"). That winter, Charles X [invaded Denmark](/wiki/March_across_the_Belts "March across the Belts") and forced Frederick to [withdraw](/wiki/Treaty_of_Roskilde "Treaty of Roskilde") from [Southern Sweden](/wiki/Sk%C3%A5neland "Skåneland"). However, a second Swedish offensive failed, and by 1659 the war had become one of attrition, with neither side able to gain a decisive advantage.

After Charles died in February 1660, his [son](/wiki/Charles_XI_of_Sweden "Charles XI of Sweden") made peace with his opponents. Sweden kept most of its gains from Denmark, but the belligerents largely returned to the *status quo ante bellum*.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

In English language, German, Russian and Scandinavian historiography, these conflicts were traditionally referred to as [First Northern War](/wiki/First_Northern_War "First Northern War").[[4]](#cite_note-Frost13-5) The term "Second Northern War", coined in Polish historiography (*Druga Wojna Północna*), has lately been increasingly adopted by German and English language historiography.[[4]](#cite_note-Frost13-5) Another ambiguous term referring to the Second Northern War is the Little Northern War,[[5]](#cite_note-6) which however might also refer to the 1741–43 war. In Poland, the term ["The Deluge"](/wiki/The_Deluge_(Polish_history) "The Deluge (Polish history)") is also ambiguous, as it is sometimes used for a broader series of wars against [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"), Russia, [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") and the [Cossacks](/wiki/Cossacks "Cossacks").

## Prelude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prelude")]

In 1648, the [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia") had ended the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"), during which the [Swedish Empire](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire") emerged as a major European power. In the [Torstenson War](/wiki/Torstenson_War "Torstenson War"), a theater of the Thirty Years' War, Sweden had defeated the former Baltic great power Denmark-Norway. Sweden had been at peace with Russia since the [Treaty of Stolbovo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Stolbovo "Treaty of Stolbovo") had ended the [Ingrian War](/wiki/Ingrian_War "Ingrian War") in 1617.[[6]](#cite_note-7) Sweden had remained in a state of war with the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") since the [Polish–Swedish War (1626–29)](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Swedish_War_(1626%E2%80%9329) "Polish–Swedish War (1626–29)"), which was concluded by the repeatedly renewed truce ([Altmark](/wiki/Truce_of_Altmark "Truce of Altmark"), [Stuhmsdorf](/wiki/Treaty_of_Stuhmsdorf "Treaty of Stuhmsdorf")).[[7]](#cite_note-Press401-8)
In 1651, an unsuccessful congress was organised in [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck") to mediate peace talks between Sweden and Poland-Lithuania.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Schultz_John_II_Casimir_Vasa.jpg/250px-Schultz_John_II_Casimir_Vasa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schultz_John_II_Casimir_Vasa.jpg)

[John II Casimir Vasa](/wiki/John_II_Casimir_Vasa "John II Casimir Vasa") of Poland

On the other hand, the Commonwealth, under king [John II Casimir Vasa](/wiki/John_II_Casimir_Vasa "John II Casimir Vasa") since 1648, experienced a crisis resulting both from the [Cossack](/wiki/Cossack "Cossack") [Khmelnytsky Uprising](/wiki/Khmelnytsky_Uprising "Khmelnytsky Uprising") in the southeast and from the paralysis of the administration due to the internal quarrels of the nobility, including feuds between the king and the [Lithuanian hetman](/wiki/Hetmans_of_the_Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth#List_of_Lithuanian_Hetmans "Hetmans of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") [Janusz Radziwiłł](/wiki/Janusz_Radziwi%C5%82%C5%82_(1612%E2%80%9355) "Janusz Radziwiłł (1612–55)") and feuds among disagreeing [sejmiks](/wiki/Sejm "Sejm") who had been able to stall each other's ambitions with the [liberum veto](/wiki/Liberum_veto "Liberum veto") since 1652. As a consequence, the Commonwealth lacked a sufficient defense.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

In January 1654, the anti-Polish [alliance of Pereiaslav](/wiki/Alliance_of_Pereiaslav "Alliance of Pereiaslav") was concluded between the rebellious Cossack Hetman [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") and [Alexis of Russia](/wiki/Alexis_of_Russia "Alexis of Russia"), who was in control of a well-equipped army that was undergoing modernization.[[9]](#cite_note-10) In 1654, when [Charles X Gustav](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav_of_Sweden "Charles X Gustav of Sweden") succeeded his cousin [Christina](/wiki/Christina_of_Sweden "Christina of Sweden") on the [Swedish](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") throne, [Russian forces were advancing into the unprotected Commonwealth](/wiki/Russo-Polish_War_(1654%E2%80%9367) "Russo-Polish War (1654–67)"), and by focusing on the northeast these drew close to the Swedish sphere of interest at the [Baltic coast](/wiki/Baltic_coast "Baltic coast").[[10]](#cite_note-11) Seeing the great success on the Russian side, Sweden also decided to intervene, among other reasons using the explanation that it was to protect the Protestant population in Poland. Having a close relationship with the Prince of Transylvania, Sweden had intentions to defeat Catholic Poland. Sweden also drew the rising [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") to its side that stood in strong opposition to the Polish government and promised military support if the Cossacks would break with the Russians.[[11]](#cite_note-12) [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") sent an expedition headed by the Kiev colonel to [Halychyna](/wiki/Halychyna "Halychyna") which soon turned back due to mutiny within its ranks. The leader of the Hetmanate did not participate in the actions due to poor health conditions.

Sweden, at that time an expansionist empire with an army designed to be [maintained by the revenues of occupied territory](/wiki/Bellum_se_ipsum_alet "Bellum se ipsum alet"), was conscious that a direct attack on her main adversary Russia could well result in a Dano-Polish–Russian alliance. Also, Sweden was prevented from forming a Swedish–Polish alliance by the refusal of John II Casimir to drop his claims to the Swedish crown and the unwillingness of the Polish–Lithuanian nobility to make the territorial and [political concessions](/wiki/Concession_(politics) "Concession (politics)") an alliance with Sweden would eventually cost,[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-Oakley85-14) final negotiations in [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck") during February 1655 ended without a result.[[13]](#cite_note-Oakley85-14) Thus, Sweden opted for a preemptive attack on the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth to occupy its yet available territories before the Russians.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

## Swedish campaigns in the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Swedish campaigns in the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/King_Charles_X_Gustavus_%28S%C3%A9bastien_Bourdon%29_-_Nationalmuseum_-_19702.tif/lossy-page1-250px-King_Charles_X_Gustavus_%28S%C3%A9bastien_Bourdon%29_-_Nationalmuseum_-_19702.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_X_Gustavus_(S%C3%A9bastien_Bourdon)_-_Nationalmuseum_-_19702.tif)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Arvid_Wittenberg_portr%C3%A4tterad_1649_av_Matth%C3%A4us_Merian_dy.jpg/250px-Arvid_Wittenberg_portr%C3%A4tterad_1649_av_Matth%C3%A4us_Merian_dy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arvid_Wittenberg_portr%C3%A4tterad_1649_av_Matth%C3%A4us_Merian_dy.jpg)

[Charles X Gustav](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav "Charles X Gustav") (left) and [Arvid Wittenberg](/wiki/Arvid_Wittenberg "Arvid Wittenberg") (right)

Swedish forces entered Poland–Lithuania from [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania") in the west, and [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia "Livonia") in the north.[[13]](#cite_note-Oakley85-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Frost168-16) The [division](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)") on the western flank consisted of 13,650 men and 72 artillery pieces commanded by [Arvid Wittenberg](/wiki/Arvid_Wittenberg "Arvid Wittenberg") who entered Poland on 21 July 1655 and another 12,700[[15]](#cite_note-Frost168-16) to 15,000[[13]](#cite_note-Oakley85-14) commanded by [Charles X Gustav](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav_of_Sweden "Charles X Gustav of Sweden") who followed in August, while the [division](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)") on the northern flank consisted of 7,200 men commanded by [Magnus De la Gardie](/wiki/Magnus_De_la_Gardie "Magnus De la Gardie") who had already seized [Dünaburg](/wiki/D%C3%BCnaburg "Dünaburg") with them on 12 July.[[15]](#cite_note-Frost168-16)

On the western front, Wittenberg was opposed by a Polish levy of 13,000 and an additional 1,400 peasant infantry. Aware of the military superiority of the well-trained Swedish army, the nobles of [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland") surrendered to Wittenberg on 25 July in [Ujście](/wiki/Uj%C5%9Bcie "Ujście") after the [Battle of Ujście](/wiki/Battle_of_Uj%C5%9Bcie "Battle of Ujście"), and then pledged loyalty to the Swedish king. Wittenberg established a garrison in [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań") (Posen).[[15]](#cite_note-Frost168-16)

On the northern front, [Prince Janusz Radziwiłł](/wiki/Janusz_Radziwi%C5%82%C5%82_(1612%E2%80%9355) "Janusz Radziwiłł (1612–55)") signed the [Treaty of Kėdainiai](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C4%97dainiai "Treaty of Kėdainiai") with Sweden on 17 August 1655, placing the [Grand Duchy of Lithuania](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Lithuania "Grand Duchy of Lithuania") under Swedish protection. Though Radziwiłł had been negotiating with Sweden before, during his dispute with the Polish king, Kėdainiai provided a clause stipulating that the two parts of the Commonwealth, Poland and Lithuania, need not fight each other.[[15]](#cite_note-Frost168-16) Part of the [Grand Ducal Lithuanian Army](/wiki/Grand_Ducal_Lithuanian_Army "Grand Ducal Lithuanian Army") opposed the treaty however, forming a confederation led by the magnate and Polish–Lithuanian hetman [Paweł Jan Sapieha](/wiki/Pawe%C5%82_Jan_Sapieha "Paweł Jan Sapieha") at [Virbalis](/wiki/Virbalis "Virbalis").[[16]](#cite_note-Frost170-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Janu%C5%A1_Radzivi%C5%82._%D0%AF%D0%BD%D1%83%D1%88_%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B7%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%BB_%28B._Strobel%2C_1634%29.jpg/250px-Janu%C5%A1_Radzivi%C5%82._%D0%AF%D0%BD%D1%83%D1%88_%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B7%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%BB_%28B._Strobel%2C_1634%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Janu%C5%A1_Radzivi%C5%82._%D0%AF%D0%BD%D1%83%D1%88_%D0%A0%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B7%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%96%D0%BB_(B._Strobel,_1634).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Herdt_Jerzy_Sebastian_Lubomirski.jpg/250px-Herdt_Jerzy_Sebastian_Lubomirski.jpg)](/wiki/File:Herdt_Jerzy_Sebastian_Lubomirski.jpg)

[Prince Radziwiłł](/wiki/Janusz_Radziwi%C5%82%C5%82_(1612%E2%80%9355) "Janusz Radziwiłł (1612–55)") (left) and [Hetman Lubomirski](/wiki/Jerzy_Sebastian_Lubomirski "Jerzy Sebastian Lubomirski") (right)

On 24 August, Charles X Gustav joined Wittenberg's forces. The Polish king [John II Casimir](/wiki/John_II_Casimir "John II Casimir") left [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") the same month to confront the Swedish army in the west, but after some skirmishes with the Swedish vanguard retreated southwards to [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków").[[15]](#cite_note-Frost168-16) On 8 September Charles X Gustav occupied Warsaw, then turned south to confront the retreating Polish king. The kings met at the [Battle of Żarnów](/wiki/Battle_of_%C5%BBarn%C3%B3w "Battle of Żarnów") on 16 September, which like the next encounter at the [Battle of Wojnicz](/wiki/Battle_of_Wojnicz "Battle of Wojnicz") on 3 October was a victory for Sweden. John II Casimir was exiled to [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") while Kraków surrendered to Charles X Gustav on 19 October.[[17]](#cite_note-Frost169-18)

On 20 October, a second treaty was ratified at [Kėdainiai](/wiki/K%C4%97dainiai "Kėdainiai") in the north. The [Union of Kėdainiai](/wiki/Union_of_K%C4%97dainiai "Union of Kėdainiai") unified Lithuania with Sweden, with Radziwiłł recognizing Charles X Gustav as Grand Duke of Lithuania.[[15]](#cite_note-Frost168-16) Over the following days, most of the Polish army surrendered to Sweden: on 26 October Koniecpolski surrendered with 5,385 men near Kraków, on 28 October [Field Crown Hetman](/wiki/Field_Crown_Hetman "Field Crown Hetman") [Stanisław Lanckoroński](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Lanckoro%C5%84ski_(hetman) "Stanisław Lanckoroński (hetman)") and [Great Crown Hetman](/wiki/Great_Crown_Hetman "Great Crown Hetman") [Stanisław "Rewera" Potocki](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_%22Rewera%22_Potocki "Stanisław \"Rewera\" Potocki") surrendered with 10,000 men, and on 31 October the levy of [Mazovia](/wiki/Mazovia "Mazovia") surrendered after the [Battle of Nowy Dwór](/wiki/Battle_of_Nowy_Dw%C3%B3r_Mazowiecki "Battle of Nowy Dwór Mazowiecki").[[17]](#cite_note-Frost169-18)

## Occupation of Poland–Lithuania and the Brandenburgian intervention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Occupation of Poland–Lithuania and the Brandenburgian intervention")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Territorial_changes_of_Poland_1655.jpg/250px-Territorial_changes_of_Poland_1655.jpg)](/wiki/File:Territorial_changes_of_Poland_1655.jpg)

Approximate extent of Swedish-occupied (light blue) and Russian-occupied (light green) Poland–Lithuania

Meanwhile, Russian and Cossack forces had occupied the east of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth as far as [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin"), with only [Lwow](/wiki/Lwow "Lwow") (Lviv, Lemberg) remaining under Polish–Lithuanian control.[[17]](#cite_note-Frost169-18) In late October, Charles X Gustav headed northwards and left Wittenberg in Kraków with a mobile force of 3,000 Swedish and 2,000 Polish troops, and an additional number scattered in garrisons, to control the southern part of the Swedish-occupied commonwealth.[[18]](#cite_note-Frost172-19)

In the north, the [Royal Prussian](/wiki/Royal_Prussia "Royal Prussia") nobles concluded a defensive alliance with the [Electorate of Brandenburg](/wiki/Electorate_of_Brandenburg "Electorate of Brandenburg") on 12 November in the [Treaty of Rinsk](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rinsk "Treaty of Rinsk"), permitting Brandenburgian garrisons. [Danzig](/wiki/Danzig "Danzig") (Gdansk), [Thorn](/wiki/Toru%C5%84 "Toruń") (Torun) and [Elbing](/wiki/Elbl%C4%85g "Elbląg") (Elblag) had not participated in the treaty,[[7]](#cite_note-Press401-8)[[19]](#cite_note-Frost171-20) with Thorn and Elbing surrendering to Sweden. In the [Treaty of Königsberg](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%B6nigsberg_(1656) "Treaty of Königsberg (1656)") on 17 January 1656, [Frederick William, Elector of Brandenburg](/wiki/Frederick_William,_the_Great_Elector "Frederick William, the Great Elector") and Duke of Prussia, took the [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia"), formerly a Polish fief, as a fief from Charles X Gustav. The Brandenburgian garrisons in Royal Prussia were withdrawn, and when [Marienburg](/wiki/Malbork "Malbork") (Malbork) surrendered in March, Danzig remained the only town not under Swedish control.[[19]](#cite_note-Frost171-20)

The rapid Swedish invasion and occupation of the Polish–Lithuanian territories became known in Poland as the "[(Swedish) deluge](/wiki/Deluge_(history) "Deluge (history)")."[[20]](#cite_note-Press402-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)

## Polish–Lithuanian recovery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Polish–Lithuanian recovery")]

The "deluge"[[20]](#cite_note-Press402-21) and religious differences between the primarily Protestant Swedes and the primarily Catholic Poles,[[16]](#cite_note-Frost170-17)[[20]](#cite_note-Press402-21) resulting in cases of maltreatment and murder of Catholic clergy and monks as well as cases of looting of Catholic churches and monasteries, gave rise to some partisan movements in the Swedish-occupied territory. A guerilla force attacked a small Swedish garrison at [Koscian](/wiki/Koscian "Koscian") in October 1655 and killed [Frederick of Hesse](/wiki/Frederick,_Landgrave_of_Hesse-Eschwege "Frederick, Landgrave of Hesse-Eschwege"), brother-in-law of the Swedish king. The Pauline monastery [Jasna Góra](/wiki/Jasna_G%C3%B3ra "Jasna Góra") in [Częstochowa](/wiki/Cz%C4%99stochowa "Częstochowa") [successfully resisted a Swedish siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Jasna_G%C3%B3ra "Siege of Jasna Góra") throughout November 1655 to January 1656.[[16]](#cite_note-Frost170-17) On 20 November a manifesto was issued in [Opole](/wiki/Opole "Opole") (Oppeln) calling for public resistance and the return of John II Casimir,[[19]](#cite_note-Frost171-20) and in December a peasant force took [Nowy Sącz](/wiki/Nowy_S%C4%85cz "Nowy Sącz").[[16]](#cite_note-Frost170-17) On 29 December, the partisan [Tyszowce Confederation](/wiki/Tyszowce_Confederation "Tyszowce Confederation") was constituted under participation of Lanckoroński and Potocki, and on 1 January 1656 John II Casimir returned from exile. Later in January, [Stefan Czarniecki](/wiki/Stefan_Czarniecki "Stefan Czarniecki") joined in, and by February most Polish soldiers who were in Swedish service since October 1655, had switched sides to that of the confederation.[[19]](#cite_note-Frost171-20)

Charles X Gustav, with a force of 11,000 horse, reacted by pursuing Czarniecki's force of 2,400 men, confronting and defeating him in the [Battle of Gołąb](/wiki/Battle_of_Go%C5%82%C4%85b "Battle of Gołąb") in February 1656.[[18]](#cite_note-Frost172-19) Charles X Gustav then intended to take Lwow, but his advance was halted in the [Battle of Zamość](/wiki/Battle_of_Zamo%C5%9B%C4%87 "Battle of Zamość"), when he was nearly encircled by the growing Polish–Lithuanian armies under Sapieha and Czarniecki, and barely escaped on 5 and 6 April breaking through Sapieha's lines during the [Battle of Sandomierz](/wiki/Battle_of_Sandomierz "Battle of Sandomierz") at the cost of his artillery and baggage. A Swedish relief force under [Frederick of Baden-Durlach](/wiki/Frederick_VI,_Margrave_of_Baden-Durlach "Frederick VI, Margrave of Baden-Durlach") was destroyed by Czarniecki on 7 April in the [Battle of Warka](/wiki/Battle_of_Warka "Battle of Warka").[[24]](#cite_note-Frost173-25) In the same month, John II Casimir with the [Lwów Oath](/wiki/Lw%C3%B3w_Oath "Lwów Oath") proclaimed [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") queen of Poland, and promised to lift the burdens inflicted on the peasantry if he regained control.[[19]](#cite_note-Frost171-20)

## Brandenburgian-Swedish alliance and Russia's war on Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Brandenburgian-Swedish alliance and Russia's war on Sweden")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Magnus_Gabriel_De_la_Gardie_%28ur_Svenska_Familj-Journalen%29.png/250px-Magnus_Gabriel_De_la_Gardie_%28ur_Svenska_Familj-Journalen%29.png)](/wiki/File:Magnus_Gabriel_De_la_Gardie_(ur_Svenska_Familj-Journalen).png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Alexis_I_of_Russia_%28Hermitage%29.jpg/250px-Alexis_I_of_Russia_%28Hermitage%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexis_I_of_Russia_(Hermitage).jpg)

[Magnus de la Gardie](/wiki/Magnus_de_la_Gardie "Magnus de la Gardie") (left) and [Alexis of Russia](/wiki/Alexis_of_Russia "Alexis of Russia") (right)

Further information: [Russo-Swedish War (1656–58)](/wiki/Russo-Swedish_War_(1656%E2%80%9358) "Russo-Swedish War (1656–58)")

On 25 June 1656, [Charles X Gustav](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav "Charles X Gustav") signed an alliance with Brandenburg: the [Treaty of Marienburg](/wiki/Treaty_of_Marienburg "Treaty of Marienburg") granted [Greater Poland](/wiki/Greater_Poland "Greater Poland") to Frederick William in return for military aid. While the Brandenburgian elector was free of Swedish vassalage in Greater Poland, he remained a Swedish vassal for the [Duchy of Prussia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Prussia "Duchy of Prussia").[[20]](#cite_note-Press402-21)[[24]](#cite_note-Frost173-25) Brandenburgian garrisons then replaced the Swedish ones in Greater Poland, who went to reinforce Charles X Gustav's army.[[25]](#cite_note-Frost174-26) On 29 June however, [Warsaw was stormed by John II Casimir](/wiki/Siege_of_Warsaw_(1656) "Siege of Warsaw (1656)"), who had drawn up to Charles X Gustav with a force of 28,500 regulars and a noble levy of 18,000 to 20,000.[[24]](#cite_note-Frost173-25) Thereupon, Brandenburg actively participated in the war on the Swedish side, prompting John II Casimir Vasa to state that while his Tartars already had the Swedes for breakfast, he would now take Frederick William into custody, where neither sun nor moon would shine.[[20]](#cite_note-Press402-21)

Already in May 1656, [Alexis of Russia](/wiki/Alexis_of_Russia "Alexis of Russia") had declared war on Sweden, taking advantage of Charles being tied up in Poland, and Livonia, [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia") and [Ingria](/wiki/Ingria "Ingria") secured only by a Livonian army of 2,200 infantry and 400 dragoons, [Magnus de la Gardie](/wiki/Magnus_de_la_Gardie "Magnus de la Gardie")'s 7,000 men in Prussia, and 6,933 men dispersed in garrisons along the Eastern Baltic coast. Alexis invaded Livonia in July with 35,000 men and took [Dünaburg](/wiki/D%C3%BCnaburg "Dünaburg").[[26]](#cite_note-Frost176-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Lemke_Skirmish_with_Polish_Tatars.png/250px-Lemke_Skirmish_with_Polish_Tatars.png)](/wiki/File:Lemke_Skirmish_with_Polish_Tatars.png)

Swedish King [Charles X Gustav](/wiki/Charles_X_Gustav_of_Sweden "Charles X Gustav of Sweden") in skirmish with [Polish Tatars](/wiki/Polish_Tatars "Polish Tatars") during the [Battle of Warsaw](/wiki/Battle_of_Warsaw_(1656) "Battle of Warsaw (1656)")

In late July, Danzig was reinforced by a Dutch garrison, and a combined Danish and Dutch fleet broke the naval blockage imposed on Danzig by Charles X Gustav.[[27]](#cite_note-Frost175-28) On 28–30 July, a combined Brandenburgian-Swedish army was able to defeat the Polish–Lithuanian army in the [Battle of Warsaw](/wiki/Battle_of_Warsaw_(1656) "Battle of Warsaw (1656)"),[[20]](#cite_note-Press402-21)[[25]](#cite_note-Frost174-26) forcing John II Casimir to retreat to [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin "Lublin"). In August, Alexis' army took Livonian [Kokenhausen](/wiki/Kokenhausen "Kokenhausen") (Koknese), laid siege to [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") and [Dorpat](/wiki/Dorpat "Dorpat") (Tartu) and raided Estonia, Ingria and [Kexholm](/wiki/Kexholm "Kexholm").[[28]](#cite_note-Frost177-29)

On 4 October, John II Casimir stormed [Łęczyca](/wiki/%C5%81%C4%99czyca "Łęczyca") in Greater Poland before heading for Royal Prussia,[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30) and on 8 October, [Wincenty Korwin Gosiewski](/wiki/Wincenty_Korwin_Gosiewski "Wincenty Korwin Gosiewski") with 12,000 to 13,000 Lithuanian and Crimean Tartar cavalry overran a Brandenburgian-Swedish force in the [Battle of Prostken](/wiki/Battle_of_Prostken "Battle of Prostken") in [Ducal Prussia](/wiki/Ducal_Prussia "Ducal Prussia").[[30]](#cite_note-Frost177178-31) Gosiewski then ravaged Ducal Prussia, burning 13 towns and 250 villages, in a campaign that entered folklore because of the high death toll and the high number of captives deported to the [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea").[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30)

On 22 October, Gosiewski was defeated by Swedish forces in the [Battle of Filipów](/wiki/Battle_of_Filip%C3%B3w "Battle of Filipów") and turned to Lithuania.[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30) Also on 22 October, besieged Dorpat surrendered to Alexis, while the [Russian siege of Swedish-held Riga was lifted](/wiki/Siege_of_Riga_(1656) "Siege of Riga (1656)").[[28]](#cite_note-Frost177-29) John II Casimir meanwhile took [Bromberg](/wiki/Bromberg "Bromberg") (Bydgoszcz) and [Konitz](/wiki/Konitz "Konitz") in Royal Prussia, and from 15 November 1656 until February 1657 stayed in [Danzig](/wiki/Danzig "Danzig"), where a [Swedish siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Danzig_(1655%E2%80%9360) "Siege of Danzig (1655–60)") had to be [lifted due to Dutch intervention](/wiki/Treaty_of_Elbing "Treaty of Elbing"), just 55 kilometers away from Charles X Gustav's quarters in [Elbing](/wiki/Elbl%C4%85g "Elbląg").[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30)

## Swedish–Brandenburgian–Transylvanian–Romanian alliance and the truces with Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Swedish–Brandenburgian–Transylvanian–Romanian alliance and the truces with Russia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Jerzy_II_Rakoczy.jpg/250px-Jerzy_II_Rakoczy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jerzy_II_Rakoczy.jpg)

[George II Rákóczi](/wiki/George_II_R%C3%A1k%C3%B3czi "George II Rákóczi")

In the [Treaty of Labiau](/wiki/Treaty_of_Labiau "Treaty of Labiau") on 20 November, Charles X Gustav of Sweden granted Frederick William of Brandenburg full sovereignty in the Duchy of Prussia in return for a more active participation in the war.[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Press403-32) In the [Treaty of Radnot](/wiki/Treaty_of_Radnot "Treaty of Radnot") on 6 December, Charles X Gustav promised to accept [George II Rákóczi](/wiki/George_II_R%C3%A1k%C3%B3czi "George II Rákóczi") of [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") as king of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania in return for his entrance into the war.[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30) Rákóczi entered the war in January 1657,[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30)[[31]](#cite_note-Press403-32) crossing into the commonwealth with a force of 25,000 Transylvanian-Wallachian-Moldavian men and 20,000 Cossacks who reinforced Kraków before they met with Charles X Gustav, who had led a Swedish-Brandenburgian army southwards. The following month saw the Swedish-Brandenburg-Transylvanian-Romanian-Cossack forces play cat and mouse with the Polish–Lithuanian forces, moving about all of the commonwealth without any major engagements, except the capture of [Brest](/wiki/Brest,_Belarus "Brest, Belarus") by Charles X Gustav in May, and the capture of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") by Rákóczi and [Gustaf Otto Stenbock](/wiki/Gustaf_Otto_Stenbock "Gustaf Otto Stenbock") on 17 June.[[29]](#cite_note-Frost178-30)

Due to internal conflicts within the [Cossacks](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") there was practically no participation of the [Cossack Hetmanate](/wiki/Cossack_Hetmanate "Cossack Hetmanate") in that war. Worn out from previous campaigns and requesting [Bohdan Khmelnytsky](/wiki/Bohdan_Khmelnytsky "Bohdan Khmelnytsky") to break with Sweden, Alexis of Russia eventually signed the [Truce of Vilna](/wiki/Truce_of_Vilna "Truce of Vilna") or Niemież with the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, and did not engage the Swedish army in any major battle throughout 1657 even though he still reinforced his armies in Livonia. On 18 June, a Swedish force defeated a Russian army of 8,000 men commanded by Matvey V. Sheremetev in the [Battle of Walk](/wiki/Battle_of_Walk "Battle of Walk"), however, a month later it was defeated by the Russians [near Gdov](/wiki/Battle_of_Gdov "Battle of Gdov"), after that the actions were in the nature of mutual raids. In early 1658, Sweden and Russia agreed on a truce,[[28]](#cite_note-Frost177-29) resulting in the [Treaty of Valiesar](/wiki/Treaty_of_Valiesar "Treaty of Valiesar") (Vallisaare, 1658) and the [Treaty of Kardis](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kardis "Treaty of Kardis") (Kärde, 1661). The [Russian war with Poland–Lithuania](/wiki/Russo-Polish_War_(1654%E2%80%9367) "Russo-Polish War (1654–67)") on the other hand resumed in 1658.[[32]](#cite_note-Frost183-33)

## Austro–Brandenburgian–Polish alliance, Danish campaigns in Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Austro–Brandenburgian–Polish alliance, Danish campaigns in Sweden")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Prussia_during_the_Second_Northern_War.png/330px-Prussia_during_the_Second_Northern_War.png)](/wiki/File:Prussia_during_the_Second_Northern_War.png)

Territorial changes following the [Treaty of Wehlau-Bromberg](/wiki/Treaty_of_Wehlau-Bromberg "Treaty of Wehlau-Bromberg"), compared to the pre-war situation (1654) and the treaties of [Königsberg (January 1656)](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%B6nigsberg_(1656) "Treaty of Königsberg (1656)") and [Labiau (November 1656).](/wiki/Treaty_of_Labiau "Treaty of Labiau")

Like Sweden, John II Casimir also sought allies to break the deadlock of the war. On 1 December 1656, he signed an alliance with [Ferdinand III of Habsburg](/wiki/Ferdinand_III_of_Habsburg "Ferdinand III of Habsburg") in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"),[[20]](#cite_note-Press402-21)[[33]](#cite_note-Frost179-34) essentially a declaration of Ferdinand III's intend to mediate a peace rather than provide military aid, which did not come into effect until Ferdinand's death on 2 April 1657. The treaty was however renewed and amended on 27 May by Ferdinand's successor [Leopold I of Habsburg](/wiki/Leopold_I_of_Habsburg "Leopold I of Habsburg"),[[31]](#cite_note-Press403-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Frost179-34) who agreed in Vienna to provide John II Casimir with 12,000 troops maintained at Polish expense; in return, Leopold received Kraków and Posen in pawn. Receiving the news, [Frederick III of Denmark](/wiki/Frederick_III_of_Denmark "Frederick III of Denmark") promptly declared war on Sweden, and by June the [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") army entered the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth from the south,[[33]](#cite_note-Frost179-34) immediately stabilizing the situation in southern Poland by [conquering Kraków](/wiki/Siege_of_Krak%C3%B3w_(1657) "Siege of Kraków (1657)"),[[31]](#cite_note-Press403-32) while Denmark attacked Swedish [Bremen-Verden](/wiki/Bremen-Verden "Bremen-Verden") and turned to [Jämtland](/wiki/J%C3%A4mtland "Jämtland") and [Västergötland](/wiki/V%C3%A4sterg%C3%B6tland "Västergötland") in July.[[33]](#cite_note-Frost179-34)

When Charles X Gustav left the Commonwealth and headed westwards for an anti-Danish counterstrike, the Swedish–Brandenburgian–Transylvanian alliance broke apart. Rákóczi of Transylvania was unable to withstand the combined Austrian and Polish–Lithuanian forces without Swedish support, and after a pursuit into [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), he was encircled and forced to capitulate, with the rest of the Transylvanian army defeated by the Tartars.[[33]](#cite_note-Frost179-34)

Brandenburg changed sides in return for Polish withdrawal of claims to [Ducal Prussia](/wiki/Ducal_Prussia "Ducal Prussia"), declaring Frederick William the sole sovereign in the Duchy with the treaties of [Wehlau](/wiki/Treaty_of_Wehlau "Treaty of Wehlau") on 19 September and [Bromberg](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bromberg "Treaty of Bromberg") on 6 November.[[31]](#cite_note-Press403-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Frost179-34) In addition, the aforementioned treaties secured Brandenburg the [Lands of Lauenburg and Bütow](/wiki/Lands_of_Lauenburg_and_B%C3%BCtow "Lands of Lauenburg and Bütow") at the border of [Brandenburgian Pomerania](/wiki/Province_of_Pomerania_(1653%E2%80%931815) "Province of Pomerania (1653–1815)"), while the [Bishopric of Ermeland](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Ermeland "Bishopric of Ermeland") was returned to Poland.[[31]](#cite_note-Press403-32)

## Denmark–Norway and Pomerania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Denmark–Norway and Pomerania")]

Further information: [Dano-Swedish War (1657–58)](/wiki/Dano-Swedish_War_(1657%E2%80%9358) "Dano-Swedish War (1657–58)"), [Dano-Swedish War (1658–60)](/wiki/Dano-Swedish_War_(1658%E2%80%9360) "Dano-Swedish War (1658–60)"), [Pomerania during the Early Modern Age](/wiki/Pomerania_during_the_Early_Modern_Age "Pomerania during the Early Modern Age"), and [dominium maris baltici](/wiki/Dominium_maris_baltici "Dominium maris baltici")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Frederik_3.jpg/250px-Frederik_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederik_3.jpg)

[Frederick III of Denmark](/wiki/Frederick_III_of_Denmark "Frederick III of Denmark")

The attack of [Frederick III of Denmark](/wiki/Frederick_III_of_Denmark "Frederick III of Denmark") in June 1657, aimed at regaining [the territories lost in 1645](/wiki/Second_Treaty_of_Br%C3%B6msebro_(1645) "Second Treaty of Brömsebro (1645)"), provided an opportunity for Charles X Gustav to abandon the unfortunate Polish–Lithuanian battlefields. With 9,950 horse and 2,800 foot, he marched through [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania") and [Mecklenburg](/wiki/Mecklenburg "Mecklenburg"). In [Holstein](/wiki/Holstein "Holstein"), the Swedish force was split with [Carl Gustaf Wrangel](/wiki/Carl_Gustaf_Wrangel "Carl Gustaf Wrangel") heading west to clear [Bremen-Verden](/wiki/Bremen-Verden "Bremen-Verden") and Charles X Gustav heading north to clear [Jutland](/wiki/Jutland "Jutland").[[33]](#cite_note-Frost179-34) When these aims were achieved, Charles X Gustav in September moved to the Swedish port of [Wismar](/wiki/Wismar "Wismar") and ordered his navy into the inconclusive Battle of Møn.[[34]](#cite_note-Frost180-35)

Meanwhile, Polish forces led by general [Stefan Czarniecki](/wiki/Stefan_Czarniecki "Stefan Czarniecki") ravaged southern [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania"), and destroyed and plundered [Pasewalk](/wiki/Pasewalk "Pasewalk"), [Gartz (Oder)](/wiki/Gartz_(Oder) "Gartz (Oder)") and [Penkun](/wiki/Penkun "Penkun").[[35]](#cite_note-Buchholz273ff-36) The Habsburg and Brandenburg allies however were reluctant to join Czarniecki, and against John II Casimir's wish decided against taking the war to the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") fearing the start of a new [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War").[[34]](#cite_note-Frost180-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/T%C3%A5get_%C3%B6ver_stora_b%C3%A4lt.jpg/250px-T%C3%A5get_%C3%B6ver_stora_b%C3%A4lt.jpg)](/wiki/File:T%C3%A5get_%C3%B6ver_stora_b%C3%A4lt.jpg)

The [March across the Great Belt](/wiki/March_across_the_Belts "March across the Belts") by [Johann Philip Lemke](/wiki/Johann_Philip_Lemke "Johann Philip Lemke").

The harsh winter of 1657/58 had forced the Dano-Norwegian fleet to stay in port, and the [Great](/wiki/Great_Belt "Great Belt") and [Little Belts](/wiki/Little_Belt "Little Belt") separating the Danish isles from the mainland were frozen. After entering [Jutland](/wiki/Jutland "Jutland") from the south, a Swedish army of 7,000 veterans undertook the [March across the Belts](/wiki/March_across_the_Belts "March across the Belts"); on 9 February 1658, the Little Belt was crossed and the island [Funen](/wiki/Funen "Funen") (Fyn) captured within a few days, and soon thereafter [Langeland](/wiki/Langeland "Langeland"), [Lolland](/wiki/Lolland "Lolland") and [Falster](/wiki/Falster "Falster"). On 25 February, the Swedish army continued across the Great Belt to [Zealand](/wiki/Zealand_(Denmark) "Zealand (Denmark)") where the Danish capital [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen") is located. Although only 5,000 men made it across the belts, the Swedish attack was completely unexpected; Frederick III was compelled to surrender and signed the disadvantageous [Treaty of Roskilde](/wiki/Treaty_of_Roskilde "Treaty of Roskilde") on 26 February 1658.[[34]](#cite_note-Frost180-35)

Sweden had won its most prestigious victory, and Denmark had suffered its most costly defeat.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Denmark was forced to yield the provinces of [Scania](/wiki/Scania "Scania"), [Halland](/wiki/Halland "Halland"), [Blekinge](/wiki/Blekinge "Blekinge") and the island of [Bornholm](/wiki/Bornholm "Bornholm"). Halland had already been under Swedish control since the signing of the [Treaty of Brömsebro](/wiki/Second_Treaty_of_Br%C3%B6msebro_(1645) "Second Treaty of Brömsebro (1645)") in 1645, but they now became Swedish territory indefinitely. Denmark also had to surrender the Norwegian province [Trøndelag](/wiki/Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Trøndelag") to Sweden.

Yet, Swedish-held territory in Poland had been reduced to some towns in [Royal Prussia](/wiki/Royal_Prussia "Royal Prussia"), most notably [Elbing](/wiki/Elbl%C4%85g,_Poland "Elbląg, Poland"), [Marienburg](/wiki/Malbork "Malbork") and [Thorn](/wiki/Toru%C5%84 "Toruń"). With Transylvania neutralized and Brandenburg defected, Charles X Gustav's position in the region was not strong enough to force his stated aim, the permanent gain of Royal Prussia. He was further pressed militarily when an Austro-Polish army laid siege to Thorn in July 1658, and diplomatically when he was urged by [France](/wiki/France "France") to settle.[[34]](#cite_note-Frost180-35) France was unwilling to intervene militarily, and Sweden could not afford to violate the [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia") by attacking the Habsburg and Brandenburgian possessions in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), which would likely have driven several Germans into the anti-Swedish alliance. Thus, Charles X Gustav opted to instead attack Denmark again.[[37]](#cite_note-Frost181-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Stormen_p%C3%A5_K%C3%B8benhavn_%28F.C._Lund%29.jpg/250px-Stormen_p%C3%A5_K%C3%B8benhavn_%28F.C._Lund%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stormen_p%C3%A5_K%C3%B8benhavn_(F.C._Lund).jpg)

The [Assault on Copenhagen](/wiki/Assault_on_Copenhagen_(1659) "Assault on Copenhagen (1659)") (1887) by [Frederik Christian Lund](/wiki/Frederik_Christian_Lund "Frederik Christian Lund").

When the Danes stalled and prolonged the fulfillment of some provisions of the Treaty of Roskilde by postponing payments and not blocking foreign fleets from access to the Baltic Sea, and with half of the 2,000 Danish soldiers that were obliged by Roskilde to enter Swedish service deserting, the Swedish king embarked from [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel "Kiel") with a force of 10,000 men on 16 August. While everyone expected him to head for Royal Prussia, he disembarked on Zealand on 17 August, and headed for [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"),[[37]](#cite_note-Frost181-38) which was defended by 10,650 Danes and 2,000 Dutch. This time however, the town did not surrender, and a long siege ensued. When Swedish forces took [Kronborg](/wiki/Kronborg "Kronborg") in September, they controlled both sides of the [Øresund](/wiki/%C3%98resund "Øresund"), yet in November a Dutch fleet broke the Swedish naval blockade of Copenhagen in the [Battle of the Sound](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Sound "Battle of the Sound").[[38]](#cite_note-Frost182-39)

Meanwhile, the anti-Swedish alliance had deployed an army to Denmark, to confront Charles X Gustav with a force of 14,500 Brandenburgers commanded by Frederick William, 10,600 Austrians commanded by [Raimondo Montecuccoli](/wiki/Raimondo_Montecuccoli "Raimondo Montecuccoli"), and 4,500 Poles commanded by Czarniecki. By January 1659, the allied forces stood at [Fredriksodde](/wiki/Fredriksodde "Fredriksodde"), [Kolding](/wiki/Kolding "Kolding") and [Als](/wiki/Als_Island "Als Island"). Charles X Gustav then tried a decisive assault on Copenhagen on 21 and 22 February, but was repelled.[[38]](#cite_note-Frost182-39)

## Sweden entrenched

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sweden entrenched")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Jean-Louis_Raduit_de_Souches.jpg/250px-Jean-Louis_Raduit_de_Souches.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Louis_Raduit_de_Souches.jpg)

[Jean-Louis Raduit de Souches](/wiki/Jean-Louis_Raduit_de_Souches "Jean-Louis Raduit de Souches")

In 1659, the war was characterized by Swedish forces defending their strongholds on the southern Baltic coast against allied assaults. A combined force of 17,000 Austrians and 13,000 Brandenburgers[[38]](#cite_note-Frost182-39) led by general Jean-Louis Raduit de Souches invaded [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania"), took and burned [Greifenhagen](/wiki/Gryfino "Gryfino"), took [Wollin](/wiki/Wollin "Wollin") island and [Damm](/wiki/D%C4%85bie_(neighborhood_of_Szczecin) "Dąbie (neighborhood of Szczecin)"), besieged [Stettin](/wiki/Stettin "Stettin") and [Greifswald](/wiki/Greifswald "Greifswald") without success, but took [Demmin](/wiki/Demmin "Demmin") on 9 November. Counterattacks were mounted by general [Müller von der Lühnen](/w/index.php?title=M%C3%BCller_von_der_L%C3%BChnen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Müller von der Lühnen (page does not exist)"), who lifted the siege laid on Greifswald by the Brandenburgian prince-elector, and major general [Paul Wirtz](/wiki/Paul_Wirtz_(Swedish_Pomerania) "Paul Wirtz (Swedish Pomerania)"), who from besieged Stettin managed to capture the Brandenburgian ammunition depot at Curau and took it to [Stralsund](/wiki/Stralsund "Stralsund"). The Brandenburgians withdrew ravaging the countryside while retreating.[[35]](#cite_note-Buchholz273ff-36)

In the occupied and annexed Danish provinces, guerilla movements pressed Swedish garrisons. After an uprising, [Norwegians took Trondheim](/wiki/Second_Northern_War_and_Norway "Second Northern War and Norway") in late 1658. In [Scania](/wiki/Scania "Scania") and [Zealand](/wiki/Zealand_(Denmark) "Zealand (Denmark)"), the "*[snaphaner](/wiki/Snaphaner "Snaphaner")*" led by [Lorenz Tuxen](/w/index.php?title=Lorenz_Tuxen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lorenz Tuxen (page does not exist)") and [Svend Poulsen](/wiki/Svend_Poulsen "Svend Poulsen") ("*[Gøngehøvdingen](/wiki/G%C3%B8ngeh%C3%B8vdingen "Gøngehøvdingen")*") ambushed Swedish forces. The Swedish garrison of [Bornholm](/wiki/Bornholm "Bornholm") was [forced to surrender to Danish insurgents](/wiki/Bornholm_uprising "Bornholm uprising"), with the commander killed.[[39]](#cite_note-Lockhart238-40)

In Royal Prussia (Eastern Pomerania in contemporary Poland), [Thorn](/wiki/Toru%C5%84 "Toruń") had fallen already in December 1658, but [Elbing](/wiki/Elbl%C4%85g,_Poland "Elbląg, Poland") and [Marienwerder](/wiki/Marienwerder "Marienwerder") withstood. On 24 November, Sweden had to abandon [Funen](/wiki/Funen "Funen") and [Langeland](/wiki/Langeland "Langeland") after the defeat in the [Battle of Nyborg](/wiki/Battle_of_Nyborg "Battle of Nyborg"). In January 1660, Sweden lost the [Livonian](/wiki/Livonia "Livonia") fortress [Mitau](/wiki/Mitau "Mitau").[[38]](#cite_note-Frost182-39)

Meanwhile, conflicts arose within the anti-Swedish alliance between the Habsburgs and Poland–Lithuania when the Habsburgs demanded ever more contributions while not showing the war efforts Poland–Lithuania had expected. With the [Russo-Polish War](/wiki/Russo-Polish_War_(1654%E2%80%9367) "Russo-Polish War (1654–67)") ongoing, most Polish–Lithuanian forces were tied up in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"). England, France and the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") had agreed on a petition in the [First Concert of the Hague](/wiki/First_Concert_of_the_Hague "First Concert of the Hague"), urging Sweden to settle for peace with Denmark on the terms of Roskilde, and peace talks mediated by France were taking place throughout 1659.[[38]](#cite_note-Frost182-39)

## North American theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: North American theatre")]

See also: [Invasion of New Sweden](/wiki/Invasion_of_New_Sweden "Invasion of New Sweden")

See also: [Peach War](/wiki/Peach_War "Peach War")

The colony of [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden") lay along the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"), a territory claimed but not settled by Dutch [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"). The Swedish colonists were the preferred trading partners of the [Susquehannock](/wiki/Susquehannock "Susquehannock"), who at that time were the most powerful indigenous group in the Susquehanna River valley and rivals to the [Iroquois Confederacy](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederacy "Iroquois Confederacy") further north. The Iroquois in turn were allies of the Dutch.

The Dutch–Polish alliance in Europe left its mark in New Netherland. Among the small Polish community in [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") was [Daniel Liczko](/wiki/Daniel_Liczko "Daniel Liczko"), a military officer who took part in an expedition to erect a fort in Swedish territory in 1651. Director-General [Peter Stuyvesant](/wiki/Peter_Stuyvesant "Peter Stuyvesant") named the outpost [Fort Casimir](/wiki/Fort_Casimir "Fort Casimir") after the Polish king,[[40]](#cite_note-Wandycz1980-41) but it was captured and renamed Fort Trinity (Swedish: *Trefaldigheten*) by Swedish governor [Johan Risingh](/wiki/Johan_Risingh "Johan Risingh") in May 1654. Following the outbreak of the Second Northern War in Europe, Stuyvesant retaliated. In the summer of 1655, he dispatched most of the colonial garrison to the Delaware River and led a squadron of ships to attack New Sweden. The Dutch recaptured Fort Trinity on 11 September and besieged the Swedish capital at [Fort Christina](/wiki/Fort_Christina "Fort Christina") for ten days before Risingh surrendered on 15 September.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) This effectively marked the end of New Sweden, but for a time the Swedish and Finnish settlers continued to enjoy local autonomy with their own militia, religion, [court](/wiki/Upland_Court "Upland Court") and lands.[[43]](#cite_note-44) Sweden had no further territorial presence in the Americas until the acquisition of [Saint Barthélemy](/wiki/Swedish_colony_of_Saint_Barth%C3%A9lemy "Swedish colony of Saint Barthélemy") from France in 1784.

On 15 September, while the bulk of the Dutch garrison was still in New Sweden, 500 [Munsee](/wiki/Munsee "Munsee") occupied New Amsterdam in what in known as the [Peach War](/wiki/Peach_War "Peach War"). No bloodshed occurred until the Dutch opened fire as the Munsee were preparing to depart. In response the Munsee attacked [Pavonia](/wiki/Pavonia,_New_Netherland "Pavonia, New Netherland") and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"). Stuyvesant later reported 40 deaths and 100 captives taken. Many Dutch settlers from outlying farms took refuge at [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam").[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-Trelease-46)

The cause of the Peach War has been the subject of debate. The armed protest and raids may have been triggered by the murder of a Munsee woman who was stealing peaches from the orchard of a Dutch colonist. Many historians, however, have speculated that the Peach War was orchestrated by the Susquehannock in response to the Dutch attack on New Sweden.[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-Meuwese-48)

After renegotiating land rights and securing the release of the hostages, the Dutch resettled most of their abandoned territory and constructed several additional fortifications. Stuyvesant decreed that "like our neighbors of [New England](/wiki/New_England_Colonies "New England Colonies")," the New Netherland colonists must now "concentrate themselves... in the form of towns, villages and hamlets, so that they may be the more effectually protected" against future attacks.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) Notably, the [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") colony was not reoccupied for several years. Its [patroon](/wiki/Patroon "Patroon") was [Cornelis Melyn](/wiki/Cornelis_Melyn "Cornelis Melyn"), former chairman of the [Council of Eight Men](/wiki/Eight_Men "Eight Men") and a political rival of Stuyvesant; he had been imprisoned without trial earlier in the year. Melyn and his family defected to [English New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven_Colony "New Haven Colony") soon after his release.

## Peace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Peace")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Swedish_Empire.svg/250px-Swedish_Empire.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Swedish_Empire.svg)

Territorial gains of the [Swedish Empire](/wiki/Swedish_Empire "Swedish Empire") after the [Treaty of Roskilde](/wiki/Treaty_of_Roskilde "Treaty of Roskilde") and [Treaty of Copenhagen (1660)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Copenhagen_(1660) "Treaty of Copenhagen (1660)"). The Second Northern War marked the height of Sweden's *[stormaktstiden](/wiki/Stormaktstiden "Stormaktstiden")*.

Charles X Gustav fell ill in early 1660 and died on 23 February of that year. With his death, one of the major obstacles to peace was gone and the [Treaty of Oliva](/wiki/Treaty_of_Oliva "Treaty of Oliva") was signed on 23 April. Sweden was accepted as sovereign in [Swedish Livonia](/wiki/Swedish_Livonia "Swedish Livonia"), Brandenburg was accepted as sovereign in [Ducal Prussia](/wiki/Ducal_Prussia "Ducal Prussia"), and John II Casimir withdrew his claims to the Swedish throne, though he was to retain the title for life. All occupied territories were restored to their pre-war sovereigns.[[32]](#cite_note-Frost183-33)

However, Denmark was not keen on peace after its recent successes and witnessing the weakness of the Swedish efforts. The Dutch Republic withdrew its blockade but was soon convinced by Denmark to support them again. France and England intervened for Sweden and the situation again teetered on the edge of a major conflict. However, the Danish statesman [Hannibal Sehested](/wiki/Hannibal_Sehested_(governor) "Hannibal Sehested (governor)") negotiated a peace treaty without any direct involvement by foreign powers. The conflict was resolved with the [Treaty of Copenhagen (1660)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Copenhagen_(1660) "Treaty of Copenhagen (1660)"). Sweden returned [Bornholm](/wiki/Bornholm "Bornholm") and [Trøndelag](/wiki/Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Trøndelag") to Denmark.[[32]](#cite_note-Frost183-33) The treaty of 1660 established political borders between Denmark, Sweden and Norway which have lasted to the present day, and secured the Swedish *[dominium maris baltici](/wiki/Dominium_maris_baltici "Dominium maris baltici")*.

Russia, still engaged in the [Russo-Polish War (1654–1667)](/wiki/Russo-Polish_War_(1654%E2%80%931667) "Russo-Polish War (1654–1667)"), settled its dispute with Sweden in the [Treaty of Cardis](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cardis "Treaty of Cardis"), which restored Russian-occupied Swedish territory to Sweden.[[32]](#cite_note-Frost183-33)

### List of peace treaties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_War_of_1655%E2%80%931660&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: List of peace treaties")]

* [Treaty of Königsberg](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%B6nigsberg_(1656) "Treaty of Königsberg (1656)"): Sweden and Brandenburg–Prussia (1656, superseded by Bromberg and Oliva)
* [Treaty of Bromberg](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bromberg "Treaty of Bromberg"): Brandenburg–Prussia and Poland–Lithuania
* [Treaty of Roskilde](/wiki/Treaty_of_Roskilde "Treaty of Roskilde"): Sweden and Denmark-Norway (1658, superseded by Copenhagen)
* [Treaty of Oliva](/wiki/Treaty_of_Oliva "Treaty of Oliva"): Sweden and Brandenburg–Prussia, Austria and Poland–Lithuania (1660)
* [Treaty of Copenhagen](/wiki/Treaty_of_Copenhagen_(1660) "Treaty of Copenhagen (1660)"): Sweden and Denmark–Norway (1660)
* [Treaty of Cardis](/wiki/Treaty_of_Cardis "Treaty of Cardis"): Sweden and Russia (1661)
